text:
The negative consequences of the meta-production caused by the industrial revolution and its benefits on the global level of welfare are well known. These include: exhausting natural resources, relying on fossil fuels and unequal income distribution. The need for sustainable development and balanced environment and economy is becoming more global. Green jobs refer to new employment opportunities arising from the transition to an economy that is sustainable, low-carbon, resource-efficient and socially inclusive. Green jobs are developed through the use of environmentally sustainable methods and practices that offer workers better working conditions, higher wages and greater access to social protection.

The present study aims to investigate the impacts of green jobs on labor market in the post-1980 period and over time in a multi-dimensional manner. Green jobs impact labor market through four channels: employment opportunities generated in the production of green goods and services, changes in the structure of employment through the process of job substitution, job destruction and the transformation of existing jobs. Green jobs not only serve to address the ecological and environmental needs of the society but also to address the growing employment challenges in the sectors of alternative energy production, recycling and energy-saving. The study also draws attention to the changing labor market requirements and hence, the necessity of the adjustment of the education system and vocational training to avoid the “green skills gap.” Finally, the study investigates the potential of green employment in Turkey with a focus on the alternative energy sector. It is concluded that while the relevant regulations in this regard are being improved, more detailed information is required on the green employment opportunities in Turkey, and a more comprehensive strategy needs to be formulated in order to reveal the full potential of the green economy on employment and to realize the associated positive impacts on the economy and society.
